questions.question: Explain the concept of User-Defined Exceptions in C++. Why do we need to create custom exception classes instead of using standard exceptions alone?
keyPoints: User-defined exceptions allow programmers to create custom error types specific to application logic
They provide more meaningful error information and better error handling granularity
Created by defining classes that inherit from std::exception or its derived classes
Enable categorization of errors specific to domain/business logic

questions.question: Write a C++ program to create a user-defined exception class 'InvalidAgeException' that is thrown when a person enters an age less than 0 or greater than 150. Demonstrate its usage in main().
keyPoints: Define a class inheriting from std::exception or std::runtime_error
Override the what() method to return custom error message
Use throw keyword to raise the exception when invalid age is entered
Implement try-catch block to handle the custom exception

questions.question: Create a complete C++ program that implements a custom exception hierarchy for a Bank Account system. Include: (a) A base exception class 'BankException', (b) Derived classes 'InsufficientFundsException' and 'InvalidTransactionException', (c) A BankAccount class with deposit and withdraw methods, (d) Demonstrate exception handling with multiple catch blocks.
keyPoints: Create base class inheriting from std::exception with virtual what() method
Implement derived exception classes for specific error scenarios
Use exception specifications and proper exception handling flow
Demonstrate type-safe exception handling with catch blocks in order from derived to base

questions.question: Discuss Exception Safety in C++ with respect to User-Defined Exceptions. Explain the three levels of exception safety (basic, strong, no-throw) with practical examples. How does RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) help in writing exception-safe code?
keyPoints: Basic guarantee: no leaks, no corrupted data, object remains in valid state
Strong guarantee: operation either succeeds completely or has no effect
No-throw guarantee: operations promise never to throw exceptions
RAII uses constructors/destructors to automatically manage resources, ensuring cleanup even when exceptions occur
